items.correct: The absolute oneness and indivisibility of Allah — the most fundamental Islamic belief. Its opposite, shirk (associating partners with God), is the gravest sin.
items.misconception: Tawhid is the Islamic word for the Trinity.
items.random_wrong: Tawhid is the name of an Islamic prayer position.
items.why: Tawhid is the strict denial of any plurality in God — directly contrasting with Trinitarian Christianity. Students sometimes invert the meaning entirely. The Quran's emphasis on Tawhid shapes Islamic theology, art (no images) and ethics.

items.q: What does the cosmological argument try to prove?
items.correct: That the existence of the universe requires a first cause or necessary being — usually identified with God — to explain why anything exists at all.
items.misconception: The cosmological argument proves who created life on Earth.
items.random_wrong: The cosmological argument proves the Big Bang happened.
items.why: Students mix up cosmology (science) with the cosmological argument (philosophy). The argument is about why anything exists at all, not the specific origins of life or the Big Bang.

items.q: What does the Abrahamic covenant in Judaism involve?
items.correct: God's promise to Abraham of land, descendants and blessing, in return for faithfulness — the foundational covenant marking Jews as a covenant people with responsibilities, not privileges.
items.misconception: Being 'chosen' means Jews believe they are superior to other peoples.
items.random_wrong: The covenant means Jews must not interact with non-Jews.
items.why: Chosenness in Jewish theology means chosen for responsibility (following Torah, being a 'light to the nations'), not chosen for favour. Students misread it as ethnic supremacy, missing the obligation it imposes.

items.q: Which deity is part of the Trimurti as the destroyer?
items.why: It's Shiva. “Brahma” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many avatars does Vishnu traditionally have (dashavatara)?
items.why: Correct answer: Ten. “Three” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many varnas are there in the classical fourfold order?
items.why: The real figure is 4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
